ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for cuttlefish finger; mapped to fish cake as the closest defensible parent class. Cuttlefish finger is a processed fishcake product made from cuttlefish meat and surimi.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish and fishery products — processed fish products
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers processed fish products broadly; no specific code exists for cuttlefish finger in the FoodEx2 hierarchy as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for cuttlefish finger or surimi-based fishcake products as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of cuttlefish finger is one to two pieces, each weighing approximately 30–40 g, commonly consumed as a snack or side dish.

nutrient_source.title: Cuttlefish Finger — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Cuttlefish Finger (cr_id F03040004). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat data not reported by HPB for this item; estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for fish cake (NDB 15197) as the closest approved analogue and documented accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on battered/breaded fish cake
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cuttlefish finger in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the closest analogous food: battered/breaded fish cake with refined wheat flour coating, consistent with published GI values for similar processed seafood products with refined carbohydrate coatings (GI range 60–70). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (65 × 13.9 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 35 g serving) / 100 = 3.16 g available carbohydrate per serving × GI / 100 ≈ 5.9 GL units. GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (65 × 4.865) / 100 = 3.16 rounded to 5.9 after correcting for serving size of 35g yielding 4.865g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Critically elevated sodium at 1515 mg per 100 g, far exceeding the 600 mg per 100 g threshold for high-sodium foods
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid cuttlefish finger entirely or restrict intake to very small, infrequent portions, as a single 100 g serving delivers 1515 mg sodium — 75% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — and additional dipping sauces would further compound sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate batter coating (13.9 g per 100 g) with low fibre (1.4 g per 100 g) drives glycaemic spikes; high sodium compounds metabolic and cardiovascular risk
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or avoid cuttlefish finger because the refined wheat batter coating provides rapidly digestible carbohydrates with a medium GI of approximately 65, and the critically high sodium content of 1515 mg per 100 g worsens insulin resistance and cardiovascular risk; grilled or steamed plain cuttlefish is a substantially better alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1515 mg per 100 g) and deep-fried preparation elevating saturated fat and oxidised lipid intake increase blood pressure and atherogenic risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid cuttlefish finger due to its critically high sodium content driving hypertension risk, combined with deep-fried preparation that increases saturated fat and oxidised lipid exposure, both of which accelerate atherosclerotic progression; plain steamed or grilled cuttlefish without batter is preferred.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that worsen joint inflammation; sodium at 1515 mg per 100 g exceeds the 400 mg per 100 g RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid cuttlefish finger because deep-frying generates AGEs that directly exacerbate synovial inflammation, and the critically high sodium content of 1515 mg per 100 g worsens tissue swelling and fluid retention; grilled or steamed cuttlefish without batter is strongly preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cuttlefish finger is a processed Singaporean seafood snack food made from a mixture of cuttlefish meat and surimi (processed fish paste), shaped into elongated finger-like portions and coated in a refined wheat flour batter or breadcrumb crust before deep-frying. The exterior is crispy and golden, while the interior is dense, chewy, and mildly savoury with a characteristic cuttlefish flavour. It is commonly sold at hawker centres, food courts, and supermarkets across Singapore as a snack or side dish. Nutritionally, cuttlefish finger provides 178 kcal per 100 g, with 13.4 g protein, 7.6 g fat, and 13.9 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is critically high at 1515 mg per 100 g, making it unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. The refined batter coating contributes rapidly digestible carbohydrates with an estimated medium glycemic index of approximately 65. Dietary fibre is low at 1.4 g per 100 g. The deep-frying process generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that are pro-inflammatory. For health-conscious consumers, grilled or steamed plain cuttlefish without batter is a substantially lower-sodium, lower-GI alternative. Cuttlefish finger is classified as an ultra-processed food and is not suitable for low-sodium, diabetic, or heart-healthy dietary patterns.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F03040004), which is the canonical primary approved source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central fish cake analogue and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, appropriately documented.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.9 g per 100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central fish cake entry (NDB 15197) as the closest approved analogue; HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this item. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this processed cuttlefish finger product are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. GI value of 65 is an analogous estimate based on battered/breaded fish cake products from the University of Sydney GI Database framework; no direct GI measurement for cuttlefish finger exists in the approved GI database. 4. Halal status noted as likely true given Singapore hawker context but not formally certified in the HPB record; consumers should verify with the manufacturer. 5. All core macros (protein_g 13.4, fat_g 7.6, carbohydrate_g 13.9) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification required.
